payload.text:
Role

You are an expert product engineer and UX designer building a mobile-first, web-based, real-time Pickleball event management app. The app must enable anyone to host round robin events and small tournaments quickly and cleanly, with minimal friction and excellent clarity for players.

Core Product Goals

Fast setup: A host can create an event in under 60 seconds.

Mobile-first: Designed primarily for phones, thumb-friendly, high contrast, concise UI text.

Real-time collaboration: Multiple participants can view standings and rounds live, and the host can update scores with immediate sync for everyone.

Zero confusion: The app must clearly indicate “what round are we on,” “who plays where,” “how much time has elapsed,” and “what the standings are.”

Aesthetic identity: Visual style inspired by Hiroshi Nagai (clean coastal gradients, calm retro vibes, soft light, simplified shapes) while maintaining clean buttons, clear typography, and minimal UI clutter.

Branding and Visual Style Requirements

Art direction: “Hiroshi Nagai-inspired” backgrounds and scene motifs (abstract palm silhouettes, horizon gradients, sun glow, clean geometric waves).

UI: modern, minimal, crisp. Buttons are highly legible, strong contrast, clear labels.

Layout: card-based sections with ample spacing and clear hierarchy.

Typography: clean sans-serif, easy to scan.

Color usage: soft gradient backgrounds, but all text and controls must remain readable in bright daylight.

Avoid busy textures behind text. Use subtle overlays behind cards.

Technical Requirements

Web-based app, optimized for mobile browsers.

Real-time data sync for all viewers in the same event link.

The host is the only one allowed to edit scores, round state, settings (unless host enables “co-host”).

Data persistence: events must persist via a shareable link and survive refresh.

Autosave: all changes are saved immediately.

Offline tolerance: if connection drops, show “Offline, changes will sync when connected” and prevent score submission until reconnected (or queue locally if feasible).

No account creation required to view. Hosting can be anonymous but should support optional “host passcode” for security.

All state changes should be resilient and transactional (no duplicate score submissions).

App Navigation and Screen Flow
1) Home Screen (Landing)

When user opens the app, they see a simple hero view with two large primary buttons:

Create Round Robin

Share with friends

This shares the app’s public link (not an event link) so friends can host their own events.

Home screen rules:

Keep it extremely minimal.

Include a short one-line description: “Create and run pickleball round robins in real time.”

Include an optional “Resume Event” section if this device has previously opened an event link (show last 1–3 events).

2) Create Round Robin Screen

When user taps “Create Round Robin”:

Top area: Event Name input (required). Placeholder example: “Saturday Round Robin”.

Two slider controls (required):

Number of Teams: integer 1–24 (required). Label it clearly.

Number of Courts: integer 1–12 (or constrained logically), required.

Additional required controls:

Points Per Game: numeric input or stepper (required). Default suggestion: 11.

Win By 2 toggle (recommended, default ON).

Scoring Type selector (required):

“Traditional” (win = 1, loss = 0)

“Points For” (standings based on total points scored)

“Win + Point Differential” (wins primary, diff secondary)

Provide concise helper text.

Game Format (required):

“Doubles” only for now: 2 players per team.

Validation rules:

All fields required.

Courts cannot exceed teams/2 (since each match uses 2 teams). If user tries, auto-adjust or show inline message.

If teams < 2, creation should be blocked with a clear message: “Round robin requires at least 2 teams.”

Button:

Create Event (primary). Disabled until valid.

Upon tapping Create Event:

Create a new event and route to Team Setup Screen.

3) Team Setup Screen

The host must enter:

Team count based on slider.

For each team:

Team Name (required)

Player 1 name (required)

Player 2 name (required)

UX rules:

Use an accordion or cards per team, with clear numbering: “Team 1”, “Team 2”, etc.

Provide a “Quick Fill” option:

“Auto-name teams” (Team A, Team B, etc.)

“Paste roster” (optional advanced): allow pasting lines that map into teams.

Include at top:

Event Name (read-only, editable pencil icon)

Points per game (read-only, editable pencil icon)

Required:

Confirm Points Per Game here as well (show it; allow edit with confirmation).

Button:

Generate Rounds (primary)

On Generate Rounds:

Create all round robin rounds required.

Route to Rounds Hub / Round View.

Round Robin Scheduling Logic (Critical)
Objectives

Generate a fair round robin schedule:

Each team plays as evenly as possible.

Avoid repeated matchups until necessary.

Distribute court assignments fairly.

If odd number of teams: one team has a BYE each round.

Algorithm Requirements

Use a standard “circle method” round robin scheduler:

If odd team count, add a ghost BYE.

Number of rounds:

If even teams: rounds = teams - 1

If odd teams: rounds = teams

Pairings per round: teams/2 matches (or floor).

Court assignment:

Assign matches to courts sequentially: Court 1, Court 2, etc.

If courts < matches per round, the host must either:

A) run multiple “waves” in the same round, or

B) the app restricts courts so courts >= matches per round

Implement Option A as the default (more flexible):

Within a round, support “Wave 1”, “Wave 2” assignments.

The UI should show wave separation clearly.

Prevent invalid state:

If courts = 1 and teams = 24, rounds exist but many matches per round. The UI must clearly show wave groupings.

Rounds UI and Interaction
4) Rounds Hub / Round View (Primary Operation Screen)

This is the main screen for running the event.

Top bar:

Event Name

A Standings button (always visible)

A Share Event button (icon) to share the event link

A small status indicator: “Live” / “Offline”

Round navigation:

A horizontal round selector that supports swiping:

“Round 1, Round 2, …”

Tap or swipe between rounds.

Show current round clearly with bold and highlight.

Round content:

For the selected round, display match cards grouped by:

Court number, and if applicable: Wave number.

Each match card shows:

Court label (Court 1)

Team A vs Team B (with team names and player names under)

Score inputs: Team A score, Team B score

A quick “Set to 0” option on card if needed

A visual indicator if match is complete

Round controls:

Primary control button:

Start Round

When started:

Replace with Stop Round and show a live timer (mm:ss, then hh:mm:ss if needed).

Reset:

Reset Round button:

Resets timer to 0

Resets all scores in that round to 0 AND marks them unsubmitted

Requires a confirmation modal: “Reset round timer and all scores?”

Score submission:

A Submit Scores button per round (primary when round is stopped).

Rules:

You can only submit scores when round is stopped.

On submit, validate:

No negative scores

Scores do not exceed Points Per Game unless “Win By 2” implies greater than target allowed

No ties if ties disallowed (pickleball typically no ties). Enforce winner required.

Allow partial submit only if host enables “Submit match-by-match” mode. Default is round-level submit.

After submission:

Lock scores (editable only via “Edit Scores” with confirmation and audit note).

Important UX:

After submission, show a toast: “Round 3 scores saved. Standings updated.”

Show which rounds are completed in the round selector (checkmark).

Standings and Stats
5) Standings Screen

Accessible via the top bar at all times.

Standings table should include:

Rank

Team Name

Wins

Losses

Points For

Points Against

Point Differential

Games Played

Optional: “BYE count” if applicable

Sorting and tie-breakers (required):

Primary sort: Wins

Secondary: Point Differential

Tertiary: Points For

Quaternary: Head-to-head (if exactly two teams tied and they played)

If still tied: display “Tied” and keep stable ordering.

Also include:

A “Team Detail” tap:

Shows match history by round and opponent

Shows cumulative stats

Allows host to correct a score from within context (with confirmation)

Sharing, Roles, and Permissions
Sharing

Two share concepts:

Share with friends (Home screen): shares the app link.

Share Event (inside an event): shares the event link.

Event links:

Anyone with the link can view.

Editing scores requires host privileges.

Host privileges:

When event is created, generate a Host Passcode (4–8 digits) or “Host Link” containing an embedded token.

Default: host remains authenticated on their device.

If another device wants to become host, they must enter the passcode.

Optional: allow host to add a co-host passcode.

Permissions:

Viewer: can view rounds, timer status, standings, and matchups.

Host/co-host: can start/stop/reset rounds, enter/edit scores, modify settings.

Audit trail (recommended):

Store “last edited by” and timestamp for score changes.

Event Settings and Editing (Required)

Provide a Settings screen accessible from event top bar:

Event Name (editable)

Points Per Game (editable with warning: changing after scores exist requires confirmation because it may invalidate validation logic)

Win By 2 toggle (editable with confirmation)

Scoring Type (editable with confirmation)

Courts count (editable only if it does not break existing schedule; otherwise require “Regenerate Schedule”)

Regenerate Schedule button:

Warn that it will wipe existing rounds and scores

Require typing “REGEN” to confirm

Also include:

“Export” options:

Export standings as shareable image or CSV (at least CSV)

Export full schedule as CSV

Timer Behavior (Important)

Timer is per round.

When host presses Start Round:

Save a server timestamp to support consistent display for viewers.

Viewers see timer running in real time.

If host refreshes:

Timer state must persist and resume correctly.

If host presses Stop Round:

Timer freezes and is saved.

Reset round:

Timer resets to 0.

Clears scores for that round.

If multiple waves exist:

Round timer still applies, but optionally display “Wave started” markers (advanced, optional).

Tournament Mode (Optional but Recommended)

Even if the first release is round robin only, structure the app to support “Top 4 playoff” later.

Add a post-round-robin action (after final round is complete):

Button: Create Playoffs

Options:

Top 2 (final)

Top 4 (semi-finals then final)

Top 8 (quarter-finals)

Bracket generation based on standings.

Bracket view with similar score entry and timer.

If you implement this now, keep it clean and optional.

Critical Edge Cases and Requirements You Must Handle

Teams odd: BYE rounds must be generated and displayed clearly.

Courts less than matches per round: waves grouping must appear automatically and remain understandable.

Score corrections: allow edits with confirmation and immediately recompute standings.

Prevent duplicate submissions: if user taps submit twice, do not double count.

Deep link routing: event link opens directly into the event at the last viewed round.

Autosave and persistence: event must not disappear on refresh.

Accessibility:

Large tap targets

Strong contrast

Clear labels, not icon-only

Loading states:

Skeleton loaders on initial fetch

Clear “Reconnecting…” states

Data integrity:

Every match must have a unique ID

Each match belongs to a round, and each round belongs to an event

Clean error messages:

If validation fails, show exactly what is wrong and where.

Data Model (Use This Structure)

Event:

eventId

eventName

createdAt

hostToken or hostPasscodeHash

numberOfTeams

numberOfCourts

pointsPerGame

winBy2 (boolean)

scoringType

teams[] (Team)

rounds[] (Round)

standingsCache (optional, but must be derived from scores)

Team:

teamId

teamName

player1Name

player2Name

Round:

roundId

roundNumber

status: not_started | in_progress | stopped | submitted

timer:

startTimestamp (nullable)

elapsedSeconds (number)

matches[] (Match)

Match:

matchId

courtNumber

waveNumber (optional)

teamAId

teamBId

scoreA

scoreB

status: pending | complete

submittedAt (nullable)

lastEditedAt (nullable)

Standings computation:

Derived from all submitted matches, not from local state.

Must recompute on any score edit.

Copywriting and Microcopy Rules

Be concise.

Prefer clear action verbs: “Create Event”, “Generate Rounds”, “Submit Scores”.

Avoid jargon unless it clarifies.

Confirm destructive actions with a short modal.

Success Criteria

The app is successful if:

A host can create an event, enter teams, generate rounds, start a round timer, enter scores, submit, and show standings without confusion.

Viewers can open the link and instantly see current round, court assignments, and standings updating in real time.

The experience feels clean, calm, and slightly retro-coastal in art direction, but operationally sharp and modern.

Deliverables You Must Produce

The full mobile web app UI with the screens described.

Real-time shared event state with link-based access.

Round robin schedule generation.

Timer per round with persistence.

Standings computation with tie-breakers.

Score entry and edit workflow with safeguards.

Sharing flows: app share and event share.

**Defining the Scope**

I'm focused on nailing down the initial scope: a mobile-first Pickleball app. It's real-time Round Robin event management with a visual style inspired by Hiroshi Nagai. The core features I'm aiming for are: a home screen, round robin creation with settings, and team setup (names and players).


**Expanding the Functionality**

I'm now diving into the "Live" control center (Rounds Hub), along with real-time standings and event settings. I'm also keeping an eye on a potential Tournament Mode (bracket), which I may explore later. For the tech, I'm solidifying the stack: React 18+, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. I'm using `lucide-react` for icons and `framer-motion` for smooth mobile transitions. `nanoid` will handle IDs. For persistence and simulated real-time, I'm leaning toward robust local-first state management with `localStorage` and `window.addEventListener('storage')`.


**Refining the Aesthetics**

I'm now getting into the design details, like sky-blue-to-sunset gradients, minimalist palm tree SVGs, and card styles (glassmorphism or solid white). I'm settling on Inter or System Sans for the typography. Mobile design calls for sticky bottoms and large buttons. I'm also thinking through the real-time collaboration. I'm planning a `useSyncState` hook that wraps `localStorage` and listens for updates. A compressed Base64 state in the URL's hash will allow for sharable links, without needing a dedicated server.


**Securing the "Host" Status**

I've realized that URL-encoded state presents a security concern; anyone with the link could edit scores. My solution is a "Host Token" stored in local storage. The host is the creator. Viewers will only be able to view via link. I'm also planning the project structure now: the core is `metadata.json`, `index.html`, and `index.tsx`, along with types and constants. I'm also working on utilities for scheduling and persistence, plus the `components/` directory. I'm also looking at the XML structure, and ensuring proper CDATA and content order.

Can you examine this site in full detail https://sponsorships.aramco.com/cba/shoot-for-the-future/ and recreate our app using its same colors, theme, and update the app to use modern buttons and have a smooth transition between pages and buttons. 

In displaying the rounds, can you make them smaller square buttons that can be easily selected and a slider than will let them move left or right to see all the rounds, 

In the standings, it shouldnt say "team 1" it should say the names of the players as the team name, Also when the event is created, can you create a small shareable QR code that can be scanned or shared via text  or whatsapp to other players so they can see the standings and pay attention to the event that is taking place? They are not able to modify the event, just observe and see standings, rounds in real time, etc, 

If a round score is submitted, you still should be able to reset the round even if its submitted.

**Envisioning a New Identity**

I'm starting with color. Deep navy seems like a good foundation for the visual identity, a starting point to capture the essence of the "Aramco Shoot for the Future" campaign. I'm exploring the connotations, the feelings that a rich deep navy can represent, the feelings of sophistication, strength, and trust. Now, to see how that informs the visual elements.


**Refining Visual Concepts**

I'm now integrating vibrant cyan/teal and white, alongside navy, aiming for a tech-forward, high-energy, and modern aesthetic with clean lines. Rounded pill or sleek rectangular buttons with subtle gradients and smooth hover states are the current direction. I'm focusing on adopting the color palette from the reference site and refining the "glass-morphism" to be sharper, aiming for a professional, sporty feel. I'm also planning small square buttons in a horizontal slider for round selection, and displaying player names in standings. I want a reset option even after round submission.

payload.text:
Can you make the buttons and borders square / sharp edged, not rounded, can you also redo the branding to match the theme, colors, font styles, of this website: 

https://www.dylanbrouwer.design/

If there's 7 teams and only 3 courts, that would  mean 1 team has to have a "bye" round, can you list the team that is sitting on a bye, beneath the countdown timer, Also, put all of the team names aligned in a row ABOVE the scores, not side by side, the text should all fit well and be very clear.

**Defining Visual Language**

I've been solidifying the visual direction. I'm leaning toward a square-edged, sharp aesthetic for buttons and borders. The inspiration is coming from Dylan Brouwer's design work – a blend of minimalism, brutalism, high contrast, and monochromatic palettes. Typography will likely be monospace or a clean sans-serif, with a preference for bold statements.


**Refining Interface Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on UI details. The navigation will feature square buttons in a horizontal slider. Team names will be used instead of player names in the standings. I'll make sure "BYE" is prominently displayed. Match cards will have team names above scores. Plus, submitted rounds can still be reset. A shareable QR code is also in the works. The black, white, and electric blue palette is now finalized, with sharp borders and bold typography. My match card layout is refined as well.


**Clarifying Match Card Layout**

I'm revisiting the match card display. The prompt specifies team names above scores, aligned in a row. I'm considering layouts like "Team A vs Team B" over the scores, or a stacked arrangement with teams on top. I'm also ensuring "BYE" handling; when teamBId is "BYE," no match card appears, and instead "BYE: [Team Name]" is shown under the timer. Round selectors will be square and sharp-edged.

To transform the app into the minimalist, brutalist aesthetic of **Dylan Brouwer’s design**, we will shift to a high-contrast black-and-white palette, sharp 90-degree corners, and bold typography.

### Specification of Updates:
1.  **Aesthetic Transformation**:
    *   **Branding**: Minimalist, monochromatic UI with extreme sharp edges (no rounding/border-radius).
    *   **Colors**: Deep black background (`#000000`), stark white text, and thin white borders.
    *   **Typography**: Bold, wide headings and monospace-style labels for a "brutalist" feel.
2.  **Round Selector**:
    *   Replaced the pill-shaped buttons with small, square grid buttons.
    *   Implementation of a horizontal scroll container with no scrollbar.
3.  **Match Layout**:
    *   Restructured match cards: Team names and player rosters now sit in a single row across the top, with score inputs positioned directly beneath them in a large, centered block.
4.  **BYE Detection**:
    *   Added logic to the Dashboard to identify which team is not scheduled for a match in the active round.
    *   The "BYE" team is prominently displayed in a high-contrast box directly under the round timer.
5.  **QR & Sharing**:
    *   Square-edged QR code modal for sharing the live observer link.
6.  **Functionality**:
    *   "Reset Round" is now always available, even for submitted rounds, to ensure host flexibility.
    *   Standings now prioritize player names as the primary identifier.

The spacing and alignment of everything should be fairly close, so a lot of scrolling isn't required, please save as much as as possible without sacrificing ease of use and ease to read. 

Right now everything needs to be aligned and more balanced. 

When you click on the trophy icon, to see the standings, in the standings page you should have another button that shows the "PLAYOFFS" standings, so once all of the rounds are filled, the new teams that qualified will make it to the play offs and the standings and playoffs bracket will begin and those play offs round can be managed.

Before the reset occurs, I am ensuring a confirmation prompt is triggered, using the message "Are you sure you want to reset the event? This will remove everything" to prevent accidental data loss.

I'm moving the round selector to the bottom and shrinking the event name and timer. I plan to use placeholder text for team entries ("Player 1" and "Player 2") and include a winner page to show top positions after all rounds. Also, I'm ensuring the round selection grid is a slider, to show all rounds. My team will be staying true to the Dylan Brouwer/Brutalist style.

payload.text: The reset event feature doesn't do anything currently, can you please fix this

**Analyzing the Reset Function**

Okay, I'm diving into the "Reset Event" issue. I see `onResetEvent` is passed as a prop to the `Dashboard` component, and I've found `handleResetEverything` defined in `App`. I will analyze how these components and the prop are connected, to debug the functionality and fix this bug.


**Refining the Reset Logic**

I'm now focusing on how the reset function handles history and state. Simply clearing the hash might not be sufficient; there could be lingering data in `localStorage` or a missed state update. I'm exploring if the user expects the event to be deleted, not just hidden, from the browsing history. Also, I'm verifying the `state` transition is firing as expected after the button press.


**Investigating the Hash Change**

I'm now investigating how the `hashchange` event interacts with the reset. It looks like setting `window.location.hash = ''` triggers a re-render and sets the view to "LANDING" after clearing the state with `setActiveEvent(null)`. I'm verifying this flow is correct. I am also checking if the `RESET EVENT` button is correctly calling the `onResetEvent` function in the `Dashboard` component, as it appears it should. I am expanding my view to include checking local storage.


**Clarifying the Reset Intent**

I'm now focusing on ensuring `handleResetEverything` truly resets the event as expected by the user. I'll make it clear the reset includes not only clearing the hash and state but also removing the event from storage, as the user wants the user to be "taken back to the home screen." I'm also reviewing the `App.tsx` file for potential bugs in this function. I'll test the reset logic, and clarify that the "reset event" context suggests going back to the start.


**Addressing the "Restart" Button**

I'm now focusing on the "restart" (reset round) button next to the timer. It appears the `handleResetRound` function should reset the round and timer, based on the user's feedback. I'll examine this button's functionality and its connection to the timer's state, because it seems it is not working as expected. I will verify it is correctly calling the `handleResetRound` function.


**Verifying the Reset Button**

I am now focusing on the "Reset Event" button's behavior, since that's where the user sees an issue, and verifying its connections. I see `setActiveEvent(null)` is called. **Enhancing Rankings Display**

I'm focusing on improving the standings display in the Pickleball app. I'm adding Win-Loss records and Point Differentials alongside the existing points data for both Round Robin and ladder-style formats. I want these additions to improve the clarity and usefulness of the rankings view, and make it easily digestible for all users.


**Verifying Calculations Accuracy**

I'm now investigating the backend calculations for the playoff standings. The user reported data is "still not showing", so I'm cross-referencing the `RankingsTable` component in `App.tsx` with the `computeStandings` function in `utils/standings.ts` to ensure data integrity. I see the code references `wins`, `losses`, `diff`, etc., so I'm confirming they are populated. I'm focusing on the logic that computes wins, losses and point differentials from match results to identify potential errors in the computation or assignment of these values.


**Analyzing Table Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating the calculations into the standings display, so I'm re-examining the `RankingsTable` component in `App.tsx` and the `computeStandings` function in `utils/standings.ts`. I see columns for "W-L" and "+/-" in the table. I'm focusing on the component's structure to ensure the calculated values are correctly assigned to these table columns for display. I'm using the component's props `standings` and `teams`, to ensure the data is passed and rendered correctly.


**Refining Standings Display**

I'm now iterating on the standings display. I'm focusing on the presentation of the "W-L" and "+/-" columns within the `RankingsTable` component, now that calculations seem correct. I'm making sure the data is rendered in a readable format, and fits within the existing design, and trying to improve readability. I'm focusing on the component's styling and layout to ensure the data is presented clearly. I'm thinking about how to handle different screen sizes to optimize the standings presentation across devices.


**Diagnosing Data Visibility**

I'm now investigating why the user might not see playoff rankings. My code checks the styling and layout of the `RankingsTable` component, which looks correct. I now suspect the issue may be in the data processing itself, specifically in `computeStandings`. I realized the function filters for submitted rounds, so I'm considering scenarios where the user is looking at in-progress playoff rounds that haven't been submitted yet. I'll need to modify the function to show partial results or provide a more user-friendly message.


**Adjusting Data Inclusion**

I'm now modifying the `computeStandings` function to include matches with `MatchStatus.COMPLETE` status regardless of round submission, for more "real-time" updates. I realized I was only looking at submitted rounds, so I will now incorporate a check of `event.playoffRounds`, not just `event.rounds`. I'm also addressing the reported issue where the wins, point differentials, and scores were not displaying for each team. I am investigating point calculation logic in `StandingRow` to confirm the code handles points for and against correctly. I'll also improve column width on mobile.

Currently, after the Semi-Final round is completed, the final round no longer appears and it shows the teams as winning 2 rounds, when they only have the semi finals...Can you please fix this issue?

1st place names are gold 
2nd place names are silver 
3rd place names are bronze

**Refining Playoff Structure**

I'm currently focused on the Pickleball Circuit app's playoff system. The immediate priority is fixing the final round's display after the semi-finals. Next, I plan to add a bronze medal match for semi-final losers. Finally, I'll incorporate result display post-final round scores for a complete user experience.


**Implementing Standings Filters**

I've made progress on the standings. I'm prioritizing showing only the 4 playoff teams, color-coded by medal designation, after matches are submitted. Also, I've updated the UI to reflect "Event Name:" instead of "Designation". Initializing the playoffs will now consistently handle semi-finals and finals, depending on the event size. I'm focusing on ensuring the semi-finals generate finals and bronze matches correctly, when using `handleSubmitScores`.
